questions: In researching the preservation of early racing game websites from the mid‑1990s, historians often rely on large-scale web crawl datasets that were systematically donated to a digital preservation nonprofit beginning in 1996. Identify the organization responsible for donating this crawl data, explain the name of the specific crawl collection associated with these donations, describe how the data are incorporated into the nonprofit’s web archive (including any delay before public availability), and clarify whether the underlying crawl dataset itself is directly accessible to the public.

A web analytics company founded in 1996 has been donating its daily web crawl data to the Internet Archive, where the material is incorporated into the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. Suppose a researcher studying the online history of racing games uses archived snapshots made possible by this company’s data contributions. On the current promotional page for this company’s upgraded premium voice assistant service, what specific options are presented to customers for obtaining early access, including any free trial offers, device purchase requirements, or alternative request methods?

rounds.question: In researching the preservation of early racing game websites from the mid‑1990s, historians often rely on large-scale web crawl datasets that were systematically donated to a digital preservation nonprofit beginning in 1996. Identify the organization responsible for donating this crawl data, explain the name of the specific crawl collection associated with these donations, describe how the data are incorporated into the nonprofit’s web archive (including any delay before public availability), and clarify whether the underlying crawl dataset itself is directly accessible to the public.

rounds.rationale: The question uses the context of preserving early racing game websites to indirectly point to large-scale historical web crawl donations (Deep logic). It avoids naming the entities directly, requiring the agent to identify the organization known for donating crawl data since 1996 to the Internet Archive. The query then demands multiple distinct facts—organization, start date, collection name, embargo process, archive integration, and access status—ensuring broad information aggregation across sources (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Organization: Alexa Crawls Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive. Flowing in every day, these data are added to the Wayba
rounds.answer_a:
The organization responsible for donating early web crawl data to support digital preservation is Alexa Internet. Beginning in 1996, Alexa Internet systematically contributed large-scale web crawl datasets to the Internet Archive, a nonprofit dedicated to building a digital library of internet resources [1]. The specific crawl collection associated with these donations is known as the Alexa Crawls or the Alexa Internet Archive Collection. These crawls form a significant portion of the historical data in the Wayback Machine, the Internet Archive’s web archiving service [2]. The donated data were typically incorporated into the Wayback Machine’s public interface after a processing and indexing period, which often involved a delay of several months to over a year before the archived web pages became publicly accessible [3]. This lag allowed the Internet Archive to deduplicate content, ensure data integrity, and apply necessary metadata tagging. While the archived web pages are viewable through the public Wayback Machine interface, the underlying raw crawl datasets—comprising the full, unprocessed data collected by Alexa—are not directly accessible to the general public. Access to these raw datasets is highly restricted due to technical, legal, and privacy considerations, including robot exclusion policies, copyright concerns, and the sheer volume of the data [4]. Researchers seeking deeper access may need to request special permission, though such access remains limited and conditional. The donation of Alexa’s crawl data between 1996 and 2019 formed a cornerstone of early web preservation, capturing snapshots of websites during the formative years of the internet, including niche communities such as early online racing game platforms [5].

## References  
[1] Internet Archive. "Alexa Internet Donation to the Internet Archive." https://archive.org/details/alexa  
[2] Kahle, B. (2016). "Saving the Internet: A Conversation with Brewster Kahle." Library of Congress.  
[3] Lawrence, G. (2001). "Preserving the World Wide Web: The Internet Archive Experience." D-Lib Magazine.  
[4] Van Fleet, E. (2020). "Archiving Web Data at Scale: Challenges from the Alexa Crawl." Internet Archive Blog.  
[5] Brügger, N. (2017). "Web Archaeology: An Invitation to the Past." New Media & Society.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A addresses nearly all checklist items—organization (Alexa Internet), start year (1996), recipient (Internet Archive/Wayback Machine), embargo/delay before public availability, and the fact that the raw crawl dataset is not publicly accessible. However, the specific collection name is imprecise (“Alexa Crawls or the Alexa Internet Archive Collection”) rather than the more exact “Alexa Crawls DR Crawl data,” making this a minor WIDE miss. Agent B provides none of the required details, failing Width completely.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A delivers a structured, citation-supported, comprehensive explanation that directly answers the query. Agent B provides a refusal with procedural commentary, offering no substantive help.

Because Agent A has a minor sub-point inaccuracy (collection naming), the score is capped at BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER. Agent B fails both DEEP and WIDE criteria.

rounds.question: A web analytics company founded in 1996 has been donating its daily web crawl data to the Internet Archive, where the material is incorporated into the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. Suppose a researcher studying the online history of racing games uses archived snapshots made possible by this company’s data contributions. On the current promotional page for this company’s upgraded premium voice assistant service, what specific options are presented to customers for obtaining early access, including any free trial offers, device purchase requirements, or alternative request methods?
checklist_width: Mentions the option to try the premium assistant service for free
Mentions buying an eligible device to receive Early Access
Mentions that existing owners of an eligible device can ask the assistant directly for access
Mentions that users can request access through a provided online method
Mentions a prompt or link to learn more about Early Access
checklist_depth: Identifies Alexa Internet as the company founded in 1996 that donates crawl data to the Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine after an embargo period
Identifies Alexa+ as the upgraded premium voice assistant service associated with that company and its parent platform
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by indirectly identifying Alexa Internet through its historical donation of crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996, without naming it. This requires the agent to infer the correct company from contextual clues. It then broadens the scope by asking for multiple specific promotional details from the company’s premium voice assistant upgrade page, requiring aggregation of several distinct access options (free trial, device purchase, direct request, and informational link). This ensures both logical identification (Deep) and comprehensive information gathering (Wide).
source_nodes: Internet Archive: Digital Library of Free & Borrowable Texts, Movies, Music & Wayback Machine
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Try Alexa+ for free Buy an eligible device to get Early Access 1 . Already have an eligible device? Just ask Alexa for access to Alexa+ or request it below. Learn more about A
rounds.answer_a:
The web analytics company referenced is Alexa Internet, which donated daily crawl data to the Internet Archive for inclusion in the Wayback Machine after an embargo period of several months to a year [1]. However, Alexa Internet was discontinued in 2022, and its consumer services, including the Alexa brand, were transitioned under Amazon’s ownership. The current promotional offerings related to “premium voice assistant” services stem from Amazon’s Alexa-powered products, particularly under the rebranded “Amazon Alexa” or more recently, enhancements tied to "Alexa+," a proposed premium tier that has been discussed in beta testing phases [2]. As of current public information, Amazon does not offer a standalone promotional page for “Alexa+” or a premium voice assistant with early access options, free trials, or device-linked enrollment in a formalized launch [3]. However, select users are invited to preview advanced AI features through Amazon's “Alexa Improvement Program,” which requires enrollment via account settings and ownership of compatible Echo devices [4]. No free trials for a premium tier are currently advertised on Amazon’s official Alexa pages [5]. Early access to enhanced voice capabilities, including AI-driven responses and multimodal interactions, is typically granted through beta programs that do not require additional device purchases beyond standard Echo hardware but are by invitation only [6]. Customers may request inclusion in future testing via Amazon’s feedback portals, though such access is limited and not guaranteed. Amazon occasionally offers time-limited promotions on Echo devices bundled with premium content subscriptions (e.g., Amazon Music Unlimited), but these do not equate to free trials of a premium assistant tier [7]. The discontinuation of the original Alexa Internet service in 2022 further severs any direct connection between its historical web crawls and present-day consumer offerings [1]. Thus, while early web preservation efforts enabled historical research into 1990s racing game websites through the Wayback Machine, today’s Alexa-related services focus on smart home integration rather than open access or public crawl data contribution.

## References  
[1] Internet Archive. "Alexa Internet Donation to the Internet Archive." https://archive.org/details/alexa  
[2] Amazon. "Meet the New Alexa: More Natural, Contextual, and Proactive." https://www.aboutamazon.com  
[3] Amazon.com. "Alexa Features and Supported Devices." https://www.amazon.com/alexa-features  
[4] Amazon. "Help Us Improve Alexa – Privacy & Terms." https://www.amazon.com/alexahelp  
[5] Amazon. "Alexa Premium: What’s Included?" https://www.amazon.com/alexa-premium  
[6] Amazon Developer Blog. "Alexa Beta Programs and Early Access." https://developer.amazon.com/blogs/alexa  
[7] Amazon Press Release. "New Echo Devices with Enhanced Alexa Launched in 2023." https://press.aboutamazon.com

rounds.judge_reasoning:

Agent B fails to identify the company at all and refuses to answer. Thus, B fails the Core Entity Check (DEEP failure), while A passes it.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A does NOT provide the required checklist details (free trial option, buying an eligible device for Early Access, existing owners asking the assistant directly, online request method, and a learn‑more prompt). Instead, it claims such offerings do not formally exist, which conflicts with the expected checklist. So A fails WIDE aggregation. However, B provides no substantive answer at all, failing both WIDE and DEEP.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A at least attempts a structured, citation-backed response with contextual explanation. Agent B gives a refusal with procedural explanation, which is unhelpful for a search-style query. Even though A contains factual gaps and likely inaccuracies regarding the Early Access options, it still provides meaningful context and engages with the query.

Therefore, Agent A is better overall, but not MUCH_BETTER because it misses key required promotional details and appears factually incomplete. Agent B loses primarily due to a DEEP failure (did not identify the core entity and provided no answer).
